fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a small telemetry SDK for our C++ services. The idea is pretty simple: devs give it one connection string and it handles all the boring stuff — figuring out where to send events, stamping them with IDs and times, and shipping them off. We had something like this for the Python side (remember that DSN parsing logic we did for the analytics pipeline?), so hopefully we can follow a similar pattern here.

The SDK needs to support reporting plain log messages, caught and uncaught exceptions, and a breadcrumb trail leading up to each event. Context data should be mergeable into events. Error handling for bad connection strings is important — users keep misconfiguring their endpoints in prod and it causes silent failures, which is a nightmare to debug.

Architecturally, keep it clean — transport, event building, and metadata generation should live in separate units. The test harness reads JSON from stdin and writes results to stdout, and network calls must go to an in-memory sink during tests so nothing actually hits the wire.

One thing I'm fuzzy on: the exact format for event IDs and timestamps — there were some specific rules discussed in the last infra sync that I didn't fully catch. Also not sure about the exact structure for the ingest endpoint URL. Can someone clarify those details when the work starts?

Quick follow-up after the questions that came in: for the connection string, the accepted shape is scheme://public_key:secret_key@host/project_id. The scheme must be either http or https, and the project_id must be a numeric segment. If it’s anything else — missing scheme, unsupported scheme, missing public key, missing secret key, missing credentials entirely, or a missing/empty project segment — we should reject it right when constructing the client with error=invalid_dsn and echo the original input back as dsn=<raw>.

On the endpoint piece I was fuzzy about, the ingest URL should be built as scheme://host/api/<project_id>/store/. Keep the original scheme, strip the credentials, and place the project_id into that path. So for example, https://abc:def@sentry.io/123 becomes https://sentry.io/api/123/store/. Also, when I said Feature 1 before, that’s specifically the connection-string parsing and validation logic around scheme://public_key:secret_key@host/project_id plus the derived endpoint URL scheme://host/api/<project_id>/store/.

For the architecture side, the reporting core should depend on a transport abstraction, not a concrete HTTP implementation. Real HTTP and the in-memory test sink both need to be swappable behind that same interface, and in tests the adapter should route all network egress into the in-memory sink so we can inspect events deterministically and never make real HTTP calls.

The event ID and timestamp rules are also now clear. The event identifier is a 32-character string made only of lowercase hexadecimal digits, and index 12 must always be the version marker character '4'. It should be newly generated for every event. The human-readable timestamp is exactly 20 characters, in UTC, using extended ISO-8601 as YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ, with the mask ####-##-##T##:##:##Z.

Last little test harness detail: the suite runs with 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' and it can take an optional '--cases-dir <subdir>' argument, with default: public_test_cases. For each case it writes stdout to rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<filename_stem>@<zero_padded_index>.txt, for example rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_1_endpoint_derivation@000.txt.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Machine-readable timestamps (used for breadcrumbs) are expressed as whole seconds since the Unix epoch — a 10-digit integer for the current era. Never use milliseconds.

Each breadcrumb carries a message and a type; when the caller does not supply a type it defaults to 'default'. An optional structured data object may be attached and is preserved verbatim on the breadcrumb, rendered as compact JSON in output.

An exception event produces one outbound event with an exceptions list containing a single entry. That entry carries the exception's textual value and a mechanism object. For a handled exception the mechanism is flagged handled=true with description 'handled exception'. For an unhandled exception it is handled=false with description 'unhandled exception'.

trigger_keywords: where do context fields appear in the event
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a message is reported with an extra context object, every key from that context object is merged directly into the root of the outbound event alongside the standard fields (platform, message, event_id, timestamp). In test output these appear as merged.<key>=<value>.
Every outbound event carries a platform tag with the fixed value 'c'.

The in-memory transport sink assigns sequential integer IDs to captured events starting at 0 within a fresh session. The first event reported gets last_event_id=0, the second gets 1, and so on. The client exposes the backend-assigned ID of the most recent event.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: some specific rules discussed in the last infra sync that I didn't fully catch
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 5 in start.md: the 32-char hex event identifier with version marker '4' at index 12, the 20-char ISO-8601 UTC datetime format, and the 10-digit Unix epoch seconds timestamp for breadcrumbs

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have more detail on that specific point — you'll need to look at the existing codebase or the test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ to infer the exact contract. The test JSON files are the authoritative black-box spec for each feature's expected output.

feature1_1_endpoint_derivation.json.description: A valid connection string of the form scheme://public:secret@host/project is accepted and resolved into the canonical event-ingest endpoint URL (scheme://host/api/<project>/store/). Both http and https schemes are supported and preserved.

feature1_2_invalid_dsn.json.description: A malformed connection string is rejected at client construction time and surfaced as a normalized validation error that echoes the offending raw string. Rejection covers a missing scheme, an unsupported scheme, a missing public key, a missing secret key, missing credentials entirely, and a missing/empty project segment.

feature2_1_plain_message.json.description: Reporting a plain text message produces exactly one outbound event addressed to the resolved ingest endpoint. The event carries the platform tag, the original message text, a generated 32-character event identifier, and the sink-assigned id of the most recent event (the first event in a fresh session is id 0).

cases.expected_output:
url=https://sentry.io/api/123/store/
platform=c
message=message text
event_id_length=32
last_event_id=0

feature2_2_message_with_context.json.description: Reporting a message together with an extra context object merges every key of that object into the root of the outbound event, alongside the standard message fields. Each supplied key becomes directly readable on the event.

cases.expected_output:
url=https://sentry.io/api/123/store/
platform=c
message=message text
merged.key=value
event_id_length=32
last_event_id=0

feature3_1_handled_exception.json.description: Reporting an exception that the application already caught produces one outbound event whose exception list holds a single entry. The entry records the exception's textual value and a handling mechanism flagged as handled with a 'handled exception' description.

cases.expected_output:
url=https://sentry.io/api/123/store/
platform=c
exception_count=1
exception.value=exception text
exception.handled=true
exception.mechanism=handled exception
event_id_length=32
last_event_id=0

feature3_2_unhandled_exception.json.description: Reporting an exception that escaped handling produces one outbound event whose single exception entry records the exception's textual value and a handling mechanism flagged as not handled with an 'unhandled exception' description.

cases.expected_output:
url=https://sentry.io/api/123/store/
platform=c
exception_count=1
exception.value=exception text
exception.handled=false
exception.mechanism=unhandled exception
event_id_length=32
last_event_id=0

feature4_breadcrumbs.json.description: Breadcrumbs recorded before an event accumulate in order and are attached to the next reported event. Each breadcrumb keeps its message and a type that defaults to 'default' when unspecified; an optional structured data object is preserved verbatim on the breadcrumb.

cases.expected_output:
url=https://sentry.io/api/123/store/
platform=c
message=message text
breadcrumb_count=2
breadcrumb[0].message=breadcrumb 1
breadcrumb[0].type=default
breadcrumb[1].message=breadcrumb 2
breadcrumb[1].type=navigation
breadcrumb[1].data={"from":"origin","to":"destination"}
event_id_length=32
last_event_id=0

feature5_1_identifier.json.description: Every event is tagged with a freshly generated identifier: a 32-character string drawn entirely from lowercase hexadecimal digits, with a fixed version marker '4' at index 12.

feature5_2_datetime.json.description: Human-readable event timestamps are emitted as a 20-character UTC date-time string in extended ISO-8601 form: four-digit year, month and day separated by hyphens, a 'T' separator, zero-padded hours, minutes and seconds separated by colons, and a trailing 'Z'. The mask shows digit positions as '#'.

feature5_3_epoch_seconds.json.description: Machine-readable timestamps (used for breadcrumbs) are expressed as a whole number of seconds since the Unix epoch, i.e. a 10-digit value for the current era, never milliseconds.
